nodes.name: Greg Mitchell
nodes.description: Stable at work, managing a team. Works long hours. Relationship with Drew is built on a shared language of competence and follow-through. Attended one of Drew's tournaments — sat in the bleachers, didn't cheer loudly, but was there. That mattered.

nodes.name: Karen Mitchell
nodes.description: Real estate career thriving. Megan is now 17 and the primary source of parental anxiety (driving, boys, college applications). Drew remains the easy child. Karen drives him to the gym, asks about school, accepts 'fine' as an answer because it's always been true enough.

nodes.name: Megan Mitchell
nodes.description: 17, senior at Plano Senior High. College applications consuming the household. The age gap and temperamental difference mean Drew and Megan exist in parallel more than they interact. But Drew has noticed that Megan's boyfriend drama — the fights, the tears, the door-slamming — gives him a preview of what emotional volatility looks like when it's not managed. He's studying it without knowing he's studying it.

nodes.name: Carol Novak
nodes.description: Still the most important relationship in Drew's inner life. The Saturday visits have never stopped. Carol met Marcus and approved — quietly, the way she does everything. She gives Drew books that are always slightly above his level and trusts him to grow into them. She's the only person who gets the unfiltered version.

nodes.name: Connor Huang
nodes.description: Fully embedded in the skater group. He and Drew still nod in the hallway. They're friendly without being friends. The drift completed sometime in seventh grade without a fight or a conversation — just the slow divergence of two people who shared a childhood but not an adolescence.

nodes.name: Kyle Briggs
nodes.description: Bigger now — football team, weight room. Part of the athlete faction that dominates eighth-grade social hierarchy. He and Drew have had no direct conflict since elementary school, but Kyle is part of the power structure that eighth grade is about to sharpen.

nodes.name: Marcus Webb
nodes.description: Drew's closest peer and intellectual partner. Three years of building together — from ugly HTML to a functioning message board for Tran's gym. Marcus handles hardware and networking; Drew handles software and systems architecture. Marcus has an easy social warmth that Drew observes and respects but doesn't try to replicate. They are fully honest with each other about thinking, though neither is particularly honest about feeling. First friend Drew has had where being wrong costs nothing. Sofia Reyes once laughed at something Marcus said in the hallway — Drew clocked it.

nodes.name: Mrs. Halverson
nodes.description: Sixth-grade homeroom. Remembered fondly as the calm room. Drew occasionally sees her in the hallway and she calls him 'friend' and he means it when he smiles back.

nodes.name: Mr. Tran
nodes.description: Three years of training under Mr. Tran. The relationship has deepened from instructor-student to something closer to mentor-apprentice. Mr. Tran still communicates primarily through correction and the occasional nod, but the trust is real. He pushed Drew into competition, adjusted training when Drew joined school wrestling, and has started letting Drew help teach the younger kids' fundamentals class — a mark of respect in the gym's hierarchy.

nodes.name: Coach Davis
nodes.description: Jasper Middle School wrestling coach. Runs practice like a drill sergeant. Recognized Drew's cross-training advantage — the hip movement from muay thai, the ground instincts from jiu-jitsu. Respects Drew's work ethic. Another authority figure who communicates through the language of effort.

nodes.name: Sofia Reyes
nodes.description: Honors track since sixth grade. Plays violin in orchestra. Quiet and deliberate — not shy, but selective; she chooses her words. Says sharp, insightful things in English class that catch teachers off guard. Drew has been tracking her presence in rooms without consciously deciding to since seventh grade. She is hallway-familiar to him — mutual awareness, nods, no real conversation yet. She once laughed at something Marcus said in the hallway, which produced something adjacent to jealousy in Drew. As of the Sunday before eighth grade, Drew has written her a letter. She does not yet know he exists in this way.

edges.visibility: moderate — Greg works long hours but attended a tournament. Their connection is through shared competence values.
edges.information_flow: indirect but warming — Greg now has the martial arts as a direct channel. He asks about training. Drew answers with more than 'fine.'

edges.visibility: high — Karen drives him everywhere, manages the schedule, is physically present
edges.information_flow: filtered — Drew gives Karen the version that doesn't trigger worry. Megan absorbs most of Karen's anxious attention now, which gives Drew more space but also reinforces his invisibility as the easy child.

edges.visibility: low — parallel lives in the same house. But Drew is absorbing data from Megan's emotional volatility as an observer.
edges.information_flow: one-directional — Drew observes Megan's relationship drama and emotional patterns. Megan barely registers Drew's interior life.

edges.visibility: high — Saturday afternoons, unstructured, fully honest
edges.information_flow: direct and unfiltered — still Drew's most honest channel. Carol met Marcus. Carol gives Drew books about things she thinks he needs to understand before he knows he needs to understand them.

edges.visibility: low — hallway nods, occasional shared lunch, but the friendship has completed its drift
edges.information_flow: ambient — they know of each other's lives through the school grapevine, not through each other.

edges.visibility: very high — two classes, Wednesday afternoons, Saturday projects, constant text/AIM thread
edges.information_flow: direct, deep, technical — they are fully honest about ideas and thinking. Emotional honesty is still limited for both of them, but the intellectual trust is complete. Marcus is adjacent to Sofia in Drew's awareness — he may be the first person Drew tells, or the last.

edges.visibility: moderate — same grade, same cafeteria, same social ecosystem. Kyle is part of the athlete power structure.
edges.information_flow: ambient — no direct communication, but eighth-grade social dynamics are smaller and more pressurized than sixth. Everyone is more visible to everyone.

edges.visibility: high — three sessions per week plus tournament travel. Mr. Tran now trusts Drew to help teach fundamentals to younger kids.
edges.information_flow: deepening task-oriented — still communicated primarily through technique, but the mentorship is real. Mr. Tran occasionally offers life advice disguised as fighting advice.

edges.visibility: moderate — wrestling season only, but Coach Davis remembers and respects effort year to year
edges.information_flow: institutional — Coach Davis communicates through the wrestling program. The relationship exists within that structure.

edges.visibility: low — hallway-familiar. Mutual awareness, nods, no direct conversation. The letter exists; the relationship does not yet.
edges.information_flow: unidirectional and undelivered — Drew has been accumulating information about Sofia (classroom observations, the violin, the laugh in the hallway) while receiving nothing back. The letter is Drew's first attempt to open a channel. Whether it opens depends entirely on how Sofia responds.

gatekeepers.gates: social access — who Drew can visit, where he can go, what activities he's allowed to do, how late he can stay out
gatekeepers.conditions: academic performance, good behavior, church attendance, being low-maintenance
gatekeepers.current_stance: broadly permissive — Drew has three years of reliable behavior. Karen trusts his judgment on most things. The main constraint is logistics (she has to drive him) and Megan's schedule competing for Karen's bandwidth. Drew is starting to feel the edges of wanting more autonomy than the driving situation allows. Romantic activity has not yet entered Karen's awareness as a variable — if it does, her stance will shift toward monitoring.

gatekeepers.gates: autonomy and responsibility — what tasks Drew is trusted with, how much independence he gets in practical matters
gatekeepers.conditions: demonstrated competence, following through on tasks without being reminded
gatekeepers.current_stance: wide open — Greg treats Drew as functionally reliable. The martial arts, the school performance, the computer projects — Greg sees a kid who handles his business. Trust is at its highest point.

gatekeepers.gates: access to reciprocal connection — whether the channel Drew opened with the letter gets a response, and what kind
gatekeepers.conditions: her own interest and readiness, the quality of what Drew said in the letter, timing, social context around receiving it
gatekeepers.current_stance: unknown — Sofia does not yet know the letter exists. Her response will determine whether this edge becomes bidirectional or collapses back to ambient.

rewarded.reward: trust, expanded autonomy, pride currency from Greg, relief from Karen's worry
delegation of responsibility (Greg), being left alone to think (Carol), not triggering anxiety (Karen)
tournament results, physical confidence, respect from the gym and the wrestling team. This now partially satisfies the sports-participation norm that Drew previously sidestepped.
social invisibility — the kind that means adults don't worry about you

EMERGING — at 14, having a girlfriend or at least a pursuit story is beginning to function as social currency among peers. Being liked by someone or liking someone is no longer embarrassing; it's proof of being a real person in the peer social order.

punished.cost: anxiety spike in Karen, uncomfortable silence from Greg, social embarrassment. Drew has internalized this so thoroughly that punishment is rarely needed — he self-polices.
loss of autonomy, increased monitoring, Karen's worry shifting from background to foreground
loss of trust, which for Drew is loss of the currency he values most
EMERGING — eighth grade's hardening social factions are beginning to punish the space between groups. Drew's drifter strategy may start carrying a cost it didn't carry before.

punished.behavior: romantic rejection — public or widely known
punished.cost: EMERGING — if the letter reaches the wrong audience before Sofia responds, or if Sofia's response is unkind and circulates, the social cost is real. The honors track is a small ecosystem. Everyone in it will know by the end of the first week.
